content:
4 min read
Just now

--

How a repetitive production problem became react-bulk-excel-import — a one-line bulk Excel/CSV importer for React.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The problem every SaaS developer knows
If you’ve ever built an admin panel, you’ve heard this request:
“Can we upload all the users from an Excel file instead of adding them one by one?”
Sounds simple. It never is.
I work on a multi-tenant society management SaaS. Admins needed to onboard hundreds of residents and flats at once. Typing them one by one into a form? Nobody was going to do that.
So I built a bulk Excel import. And that’s when I discovered how deep this rabbit hole goes.
What “just add Excel import” actually means
Here’s everything hiding behind that one-line requirement:
1. File parsing. Reading .xlsx, .xls, and .csv files in the browser with SheetJS, handling empty rows, merged cells, and weird formatting.
2. Header matching. Your code expects email. The file says Email. The next file says E-Mail ID. Another one has a trailing space. Real users never follow your template exactly.
3. Validation. Required fields, format checks (is this actually an email?), type conversion (Excel numbers arrive as strings), and cross-row rules like duplicate detection within the file itself.
4. Error feedback. If row 47 has a bad phone number, the admin needs to see exactly that — row 47, phone column, and why it failed. A generic “import failed” message means angry support tickets.
5. Batching and progress. You can’t POST 5,000 rows in one request. You need batches, a progress bar, and graceful handling when batch 12 of 40 fails.
6. The UI for all of the above. Drop zone, preview table, red/green row highlighting, template download, done screen.
By the time it worked properly, it was ~300 lines of code. And here’s the kicker: two months later, another module needed the same thing. Then a third one.
Copy-pasting 300 lines and tweaking column names three times — that was my signal. This shouldn’t live inside one project. It should be a library.
The solution: one component, your columns, your API call
I extracted the whole thing into an open-source npm package:
npm install react-bulk-excel-importUsage is genuinely one component:
import { ExcelImporter } from "react-bulk-excel-import";<ExcelImporter
open={open}
onClose={() => setOpen(false)}
columns={[
{ header: "Name", key: "name", required: true },
{
header: "Email",
key: "email",
required: true,
validate: (v) => (String(v).includes("@") ? null : "Invalid email"),
},
{ header: "Age", key: "age", transform: (v) => Number(v) },
]}
onImport={async (rows) => {
await fetch("/api/users/bulk", {
method: "POST",
body: JSON.stringify(rows),
});
}}
/>
You define your columns and your API call. Everything else — drag & drop, parsing, header matching, validation preview with red/green rows, template download, batched upload with progress — is built in. No CSS files, no extra setup.
Design decisions I’m happy with
Smart header matching. Email, email, E_MAIL, and E-mail all match the same column. You can also pass aliases: header: ["Email", "E-mail ID"]. This one feature eliminates most real-world import failures.
Three validation layers. Required checks → per-column validators → cross-row rules (like duplicate emails within the file). Each error is attached to its exact row and column, so users fix data instead of guessing.
Only valid rows get imported. Invalid rows stay in the preview with error messages. No all-or-nothing failures.
Batched by default. Rows go to your API in batches of 50 (configurable), with a live progress bar. Handles 10k–20k row files comfortably.
Headless hook included. Don’t like my modal? Use useBulkImport() — the same parsing/validation/batching engine with zero UI. Render whatever you want.
TypeScript-first. Full type definitions, shipped as both ESM and CJS.
What I learned publishing my first npm package
A few things nobody tells you:

• Building the library is 60% of the work. The other 40% is packaging: dual ESM/CJS output, type definitions, peer dependencies done right, and a README that answers “why should I care” in 10 seconds. I used tsup for bundling and it made the build side almost painless.

• Dogfooding is the best QA. The library runs in my production app. Every rough edge I hit as a user of my own package became a fix before anyone else saw it.

• Extracting a library forces better design. Inside the app, my import code was tangled with app-specific logic. Making it generic meant separating the engine (parsing, validation, batching) from the UI — which is exactly why the headless hook exists.
Try it
If your React app needs “import from Excel” — and almost every admin panel eventually does — this might save you a few days:

• npm: npmjs.com/package/react-bulk-excel-import

• GitHub: github.com/vivek1209-star-blog/react-bulk-excel-import
It’s MIT licensed. Issues, feature requests, and PRs are welcome — I’m actively maintaining it since it powers a production system.
If you’ve built this feature yourself, I’d genuinely love to hear what edge cases you hit. There’s always one more weird Excel file out there.

I’m Vivek Mishra, a Full-Stack MERN Developer from Mumbai working on SaaS and payment platforms. I write about React, Node.js, and lessons from production. Let’s connect on LinkedIn.
